speaker: interviewer
text: July 22, 1996. Mr. Fuchs, would you please introduce your wife for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tina, this is my wife, Irene.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I just want to ask you why it took you so long.

speaker: interviewer
text: We hope-- it was the most fascinating interview for us, because there aren't too many people who lived through what you lived through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I would make other plans, you know, to go out and this-- I was sitting--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Talking about--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm very, very happy that we did do it. And I'm sorry if we caused you any inconvenience.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm very happy you're both here together and that you have lived your life together after the war and you met each other. And do you want to tell us something about your children, what your children have accomplished, their professions? Would you like to tell us something?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Well, my younger son is a editor, publisher. And my daughter's husband is a lawyer. And my son is in the computing in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're hitting all the professions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're having a lot of nachas, I hope, from your family. Thank you for letting us in your home, inconveniencing you. But it was the most important interview. And I thank you both for letting us have it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my pleasure. If I can, if I can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Assist you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Something do for it--

speaker: interviewer
text: It certainly will it will assist us and the world and our next generation hopefully. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Avraham Samuel Fuchsbrunner. He was born in Poland or in Russia, in 1881. And this picture is-- I saw this picture at home when I was in my young years. And this is like a wedding picture, like after the wedding picture. My mother is not here. It was a picture for my mother too, but it's-- I don't know where it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you have this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had received this picture from my brother in Belgium. And he received from Israel from some relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older brother, Reuven Fuchsbrunner. He was born in Poland in December, 1915. And he was, on this picture, he was in the early 20's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this taken in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he is alive today?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is not alive today.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's not alive today. Where did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died from when he, when he traveled from [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE]. And the SS took him, took him off the train. And they gave him a shovel. And there were two girls with him there. And he says to them, I am digging, I am digging my grave now. And this is what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a year and a half older from me. This picture, on the center, on the left side on the center is my brother Ben. And this is a group picture from the Mizrahi. He was born in Chrzanow. 1910, October.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken-- I guess, it was in the, in the '30s, late '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my older sister, Regina Rifka Fuchsbrunner. And she was born in Chrzanow. And this picture must be from the-- she was born in 1913, in Chrzanow. And this must be-- her age must be here 12, 13 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was married before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married to Jacobovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Libig Jacobovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hannah. And her husband survived the war. And he is not alive now. But he brought her to [INAUDIBLE] Israel, her and her mother-in-law, Jacobovits' mother and Jacobovits' sister, Sprynca.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself. My name is Hersh Fuchsbrunner. And born in July, 1917. I lived in Chrzanow, born Chrzanow. And this picture was taken 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the card for?

speaker: interviewee
text: ID card-- I don't even remember. But you said-- what was it there? A membership card?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the International Refugee--

speaker: interviewee
text: Refugee card.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my wedding picture. Irene Fuchsbrunner to Henry Fuchsbrunner. And this was made in 1952, November 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Brooklyn on the wedding hall, which was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Savoy Gardens.

speaker: interviewee
text: Savoy Gardens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a tombstone taken from Krakow on the cemetery where my sister, Regina Rifka Fuchsbrunner-- Jacobovits, and her mother-in-law, [NON-ENGLISH] Jacobovits [NON-ENGLISH], and her, and her sister-in-law, Sprynca Jacobovits, which was placed in the Krakow cemetery, where she-- from Krakow, where she was murdered in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did her husband place her in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture-- this stone was placed after the war, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you take this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1992. '93? '93.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this a Jewish cemetery in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Jewish cemetery on the Miodowa Street in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it well maintained?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is not well maintained.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older brother, Ben Fuchsbrunner. And this is-- this picture is near the cemetery in Chrzanow. When we were in 1993, in Poland, by the cemetery, and that's where it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's still a cemetery? A Jewish cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the cemetery. But there is no Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there is-- they've maintained.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cemetery is maintained by a Polack. But today, there is no living Jews in Poland, in Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your brother living today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother is living today in Belgium in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my youngest son, Richard Reuven, with his wife Ann [NON-ENGLISH], and her two-- and their two daughters. One is Alexandra-Zahava Eve. And the younger one is Maxi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken somewhere. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say at least a year, year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Renee Rifka Wurman, with her husband Kenny. And they live now in Syosset, Long Island. He is a lawyer. And she is a-- she is now-- she was before-- she was an optic--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ophthalmologist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ophthalmologist-- and she is now a mother of two kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is two of my dear girls, my granddaughters. One is-- the older one is Carly Paige. And the other one is Adam--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jake.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jake. One is four. And The other one is a year and something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken here in New York. My daughter's-- my daughter Renee's babies. This is my older son, Alan, or Avrum [NON-ENGLISH]. He is now-- he was born 1956, in July. And he was married, but divorced. And he lives in Florida. And he is in computing. And hopefully, I'll have nachas from him too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: From left to right, this is Maxi in front, Carly, Alexandra, and Alexandra, and Adam Jake, my grandkids. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message you'd like to give your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I can give you is I love them very much. I care for them very much. And I have great nachas for my grandchildren. And I hope and expect more. And more is better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture speaks for itself, I guess. This is my good wife, Irene-- for many-- for the last forty-some years, 45 years?-- and myself. We were, at that time, on vacation. And we hope to enjoy life further. Where was this picture taken? Was it in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it was, yeah, in Morocco. That's right. In Morocco.

speaker: interviewer
text: In North Africa. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you before I had a trip to Spain, Morocco, and Paris. And I was in Belgium. And I was in Holland. And I was in Poland. And this is one of those pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much for sharing--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --everything with us.
